name: Monsaraz Menhirs and Churches Route
name_original: Rota dos Menires e Igrejas de Monsaraz — percurso circular  em Monsaraz
description: Hike the Menires e Igrejas de Monsaraz Route, an 8.8-mile circular trail exploring ancient megaliths and a medieval village.
region_intro: Urban hiking trails around the Guadiana offer a blend of historical exploration and riverine landscapes. The region, straddling the border between Spain and Portugal, features diverse environments including rolling hills, river valleys, and areas rich in Roman and Moorish heritage. The Guadiana River itself is a central feature, providing paths along its banks and through towns with significant architectural and archaeological sites. These urban routes often connect historical landmarks, aqueducts, and traditional town centers. Best urban hiking trails around the Guadiana The most popular urban hiking route is Mérida Roman Heritage Trail, a 2.5 miles (4.0 km) trail that takes 1 hour 2 minutes to complete. This easy route explores the ancient Roman sites within Mérida. Another top favourite among local hikers is Vía de la Plata and Los Milagros Aqueduct Loop, an easy 3.4 miles (5.4 km) path. This trail combines historical Roman infrastructure with scenic urban paths. Local hikers also love the Passeio Pombalino — circular route (PR3), a 2.3 miles (3.7 km) trail leading through urban and historical areas, often completed in about 56 minutes. Hiking around the Guadiana is defined by riverine paths, historical town centers, and ancient Roman and Moorish landmarks. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy strolls to moderate urban explorations. The routes in Guadiana are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 700 reviews. More than 3000 hikers have used komoot to explore Guadiana's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many urban hiking trails are available in the Guadiana region?
A: The Guadiana region offers over 60 urban hiking trails, catering to various preferences and fitness levels. You'll find a good mix of easy and moderate routes to explore the towns and their surroundings.

Q: What are the best times of year for urban walks in the Guadiana region?
A: Spring and autumn are ideal for urban walks in the Guadiana region due to the mild weather. These seasons offer comfortable temperatures for exploring the towns and their historical sites without the intense heat of summer or the cooler, wetter conditions of winter.

Q: Are there any easy urban hikes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, many urban trails in the Guadiana region are suitable for beginners and families. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991638966/merida-roman-heritage-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mérida Roman Heritage Trail&lt;/a&gt; is an easy route that takes you through the historic city of Mérida, showcasing its ancient past. Another great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1418079512/passeio-pombalino-circular-route-pr3" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Passeio Pombalino — circular route (PR3)&lt;/a&gt;, which is also considered easy and offers a pleasant urban stroll.

Q: What kind of historical landmarks can I see on urban hikes in the Guadiana region?
A: Urban hikes in the Guadiana region are rich with historical landmarks. In Mérida, you can explore ancient Roman structures like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3775017" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Temple of Diana&lt;/a&gt; and the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3813989" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Milagros Aqueduct&lt;/a&gt;. The historic town of Mértola, with its Moorish architecture and castle, also offers a glimpse into the region's past. In Vila Real de Santo António, you can visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4266986" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Praça Marquês de Pombal&lt;/a&gt; and the nearby &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1654225" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Iron Bridge over the Guadiana River&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular urban walking routes in the Guadiana region?
A: Yes, you'll find several circular urban walking routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1418079512/passeio-pombalino-circular-route-pr3" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Passeio Pombalino — circular route (PR3)&lt;/a&gt; is a great example of an easy circular trail. For a slightly longer option, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31684760" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Milagros Aqueduct loop from Mérida&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a moderate challenge while still being a loop.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about urban hiking in the Guadiana region?
A: The komoot community highly rates urban hiking in the Guadiana region, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 700 ratings. Reviewers often praise the region's rich history, the well-preserved ancient sites, and the pleasant atmosphere of the towns and cities along the trails.

Q: Can I combine urban hikes with visits to natural attractions?
A: Absolutely. While focusing on urban areas, the Guadiana region is also known for its natural beauty. You can find urban trails that lead to or are near natural features like the Guadiana River itself, or explore areas close to the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3568353" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Vicario Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;. The Parque Natural do Vale do Guadiana, with its diverse landscapes and wildlife, is also easily accessible from several towns.

Q: Are there any urban trails that highlight the Guadiana River?
A: Many urban trails in the region offer views or access to the Guadiana River. The river is a central feature, and you'll find paths that follow its course, providing scenic riverine perspectives. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991636459/via-de-la-plata-and-los-milagros-aqueduct-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía de la Plata and Los Milagros Aqueduct Loop&lt;/a&gt; in Mérida offers a blend of historical sites and river views.

Q: What should I bring for an urban hike in the Guadiana region?
A: For urban hikes in the Guadiana, comfortable walking shoes are essential. Depending on the season, bring sun protection (hat, sunscreen) and plenty of water, especially during warmer months. A camera is also recommended to capture the historical sites and scenic views.

Q: Are there any less crowded urban hiking options in the Guadiana region?
A: While popular sites can attract visitors, many of the 60+ urban trails offer a chance to explore at a more leisurely pace. Consider exploring the charming whitewashed villages within the Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche Natural Park, which often have quieter paths connecting them, providing a more serene urban hiking experience away from the main tourist hubs.

Q: Are there any urban trails that showcase the region's mining heritage?
A: Yes, the area around Mina de São Domingos, an old mining town, offers unique landscapes shaped by its mining history. While not explicitly listed as urban hikes in this guide, the town itself and its immediate surroundings provide a fascinating glimpse into the region's past, with fields of slag resembling a 'lunar field' and historical mining infrastructure.
tips:
The Xerez Cromlech stands on open land, close to the Convent of Orada, and with a view of the walls of Monsaraz. It is the second "home" to prevent it from being submerged by the Alqueva dam. 

The 55 granite menhirs of the Xerez Cromlech have already been to other places because they were moved here, to the vicinity of the village of Telheiro, in 2004, to prevent submersion by the Alqueva dam. 

The cromlech was in front of Monte Xerez, close to a dolmen, about 5km from Monsaraz.

Located near Monsaraz, the Convento da Orada stands out in the Alentejo plains. Its church is linked to the name of D. Nuno Álvares Pereira, the Holy Constable who prayed here between his battles against the Castilians. 

The Convento da Orada was built between 1700 and 1741 and housed the Order of the Discalced Augustinians until the beginning of the 19th century. 

The Convento is currently managed by the Fundação do Convento da Orada, which promotes the safeguarding of the architectural, cultural and artistic heritage.

The Menhir do Outeiro (in Portuguese: Menir do Outeiro), also known as Menhir no Sítio do Penedo Comprido, is a megalith located halfway between the villages of Outeiro and Barrada, in the municipality of Reguengos de Monsaraz, in the district of Évora, in the Alentejo region of Portugal, just a few kilometres from the Portuguese-Spanish border. 

This menhir was discovered in 1969 by Henrique Leonor Pina and José Pires Gonçalves and, on their initiative, it was raised again around 1970. 

The menhir was classified as a Portuguese National Monument in 1971.

The churchyard of Barrada, where popular and religious festivals are held. The interior of the church can be used for concerts as part of the activities programme in the square. Proximity to the Barrada Community Centre. Minimum accessibility conditions. Toilets. Spaces for adaptation into dressing rooms.

The Menhir of Abelhoa, also known as Menhir of Bulhoa, is a granite menhir located near Monsaraz, in the municipality of Reguengos de Monsaraz, district of Évora, in Portugal. In 1971 it was classified as a National Monument.

The Church of Monsaraz, or Church of Our Lady of Lagoa, was built in the 16th century and rebuilt on two occasions, once following the earthquake of 1755 and once a century later. 

This church has a façade with two towers and an ogival head. Inside, it houses a 14th-century marble tomb carved with 14 saints and where the remains of Tomás Martins rest. 

Outside, next to the church, stands an 18th-century pillory (stone pillory) crowned by a Manueline globe. Opposite this pillory is the Church of Misericórdia and, next to it, the Museum of Sacred Art.

faqs.question: What is the estimated duration for hiking the Menires e Igrejas de Monsaraz Route?
faqs.answer: The Menires e Igrejas de Monsaraz Route is typically completed in 3 to 4 hours. This estimate accounts for the moderate difficulty and the 14.1 km distance.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this circular route?
faqs.answer: The terrain on this route is varied, featuring rural roads, gentle slopes, and country lanes. You'll walk through areas with vineyards and olive trees. Within the medieval village of Monsaraz, expect narrow, cobbled streets.

faqs.question: What historical and natural landmarks can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The route is rich in history and natural beauty. You'll encounter ancient megalithic monuments such as the Cromlech of Xerez, the Menhir of Outeiro, and the Menir da Bulhoa. The trail also passes by historical sites like the Convento da Orada and religious buildings such as the Barrada Church and the Main Church of Monsaraz. Additionally, you'll enjoy panoramic views of the Alentejo countryside and the vast Alqueva Lake.

faqs.question: Is the Menires e Igrejas de Monsaraz Route suitable for dogs?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Menires e Igrejas de Monsaraz Route is generally dog-friendly. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through agricultural areas or near livestock, and to be mindful of other hikers and local regulations.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: The best time to hike this trail is typically during the spring (March to May) or autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is milder, making for more comfortable hiking conditions. Summers can be very hot in the Alentejo region, and winters can be cooler and wetter.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to hike the Menires e Igrejas de Monsaraz Route. The trail is freely accessible to the public.

faqs.question: Where can I park when visiting the Menires e Igrejas de Monsaraz Route?
faqs.answer: Parking is generally available in and around the village of Monsaraz. As this is a circular route, you can typically start and end your hike from Monsaraz itself, where parking options can be found. It's advisable to arrive earlier in the day, especially during peak season, to secure a spot.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route primarily follows the 'Rota dos Menires e Igrejas de Monsaraz' (100% of the route). It also briefly intersects with sections of 'Horta dos Revoredos' and 'Ponte dos Frades'.

faqs.question: Is the Menires e Igrejas de Monsaraz Route suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: While the route is classified as moderate and covers 14.1 km, it can be suitable for families with older children who are accustomed to hiking. The varied terrain and historical points of interest can keep younger hikers engaged, but consider the overall distance and elevation gain for their endurance.

faqs.question: What should I pack for a hike on this route?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate difficulty and duration, it's recommended to pack plenty of water, snacks, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and wear comfortable hiking shoes. Depending on the season, layers of clothing are advisable. A camera would also be great to capture the stunning views and historical sites.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay near the trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, the historic village of Monsaraz offers several options for dining and accommodation. You'll find cafes and restaurants within the walled village, perfect for a meal or refreshment before or after your hike. There are also various guesthouses and hotels in and around Monsaraz.

faqs.question: What makes Monsaraz village a special part of this route?
faqs.answer: Monsaraz is a captivating medieval hilltop village, often called a 'museum village'. Its ancient walls, castle, and historic churches, like the Igreja Matriz de Nossa Senhora da Lagoa, offer a deep dive into the region's past. The village's strategic location provides unparalleled views over the Guadiana River and the Spanish border, making it a highlight for exploration beyond the trail itself.
